caveats: a: The BLS occupation group includes several durable-goods wholesale industries, not only electrical apparatus distribution.
a: Anthropic conversations are a self-selected usage sample and do not measure realized labor substitution.
a: Wesco is much larger and more digitally mature than the target LMM firm population.
rho: No controlled AI implementation study specific to 423610 was found.
rho: Large-company digital transformation may not be affordable or portable to small independent firms.
rho: Incorrect substitutions, quantities, voltage ratings, certifications, or delivery promises can create outsized liability and require human approval.
e: Public evidence describes the industry activity and one large distributor, not the distribution of LMM firm quality.
e: The provided firm count is margin-bridged and may include firms with temporary project profits or weak normalized EBITDA.
e: Supplier authorization and local competitive structure can make apparently similar distributors economically different.
s5: Owner-intent and owner-age evidence is broad rather than 423610-specific.
s5: Supplier consent and line-card continuity can determine whether a transaction preserves the business.
s5: Branch, asset, tuck-in, and internal-family transfers may not all meet the qualifying control-transfer definition.
q: No public evidence directly measures pass-through of AI savings in 423610.
q: Retention differs between commodity wire and conduit, engineered equipment, MRO availability, and value-added project services.
q: Commodity, tariff, freight, rebate, and inventory effects can swamp workflow savings in reported margins.
d5: Electricity demand, generating capacity, and federal funding are upstream demand proxies rather than direct merchant-wholesale service volumes.
d5: AEO cases are alternative futures, not a single prediction, and project spending can lag load growth.
d5: Nominal sales can rise from copper, tariffs, or equipment inflation without an equivalent increase in constant-quality service quantity.
o: Operator requirement is distinct from the level of end-market demand captured in d5.
o: Large OEMs, utilities, and contractors can buy directly or automate replenishment for standardized products.
o: Supplier authorization, local availability, emergency response, and technical liability make disintermediation product-specific.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Product resale margin on stocked and direct-ship electrical supplies and equipment, supplemented by project quotations, technical selection, inventory availability, delivery, kitting and labeling, materials management, supply-chain programs, and other value-added services. Revenue is generated through frequent orders, bids, projects, and negotiated customer or supplier programs.
lens.excluded_activities: Manufacturing; electrical installation and contracting; pure commission agents or brokers that do not take title; retail home-center sales; communications-equipment distribution outside the electrical-apparatus basket; captive procurement operations; shells; and firms outside the stated EBITDA band.
lens.included_activities: US merchant wholesalers that take title to and repeatedly supply electrical construction materials, wiring supplies, lighting, wire and cable, controls, transformers, switchgear, and related generation, transmission, distribution, automation, and MRO equipment to external contractors, industrial customers, OEMs, utilities, institutions, and commercial facilities.

a.bridge: No six-digit occupation table was found. BLS's selected durable-goods merchant-wholesale group includes NAICS 4236 and reports sales at 22.11% of employment, office and administrative support at 15.27%, management at 9.96%, production at 7.97%, and transportation and material moving at 26.54%. Anthropic observes AI concentrated in digital tasks and physical-labor categories least represented. I assign high exposure to quotation preparation, product and specification search, order entry, customer correspondence, procurement analysis, invoice reconciliation, and document handling; moderate exposure to sales, project coordination, inventory planning, credit, and logistics exceptions; and low exposure to receiving, picking, loading, local delivery, fabrication, testing, and field support. The broad BLS group can misstate the technical-sales and warehouse mix of electrical distributors, so the interval is wide.
caveats: The BLS occupation group includes several durable-goods wholesale industries, not only electrical apparatus distribution.
Anthropic conversations are a self-selected usage sample and do not measure realized labor substitution.
Wesco is much larger and more digitally mature than the target LMM firm population.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted 423610 task and time study separating inside and outside sales, quotations, purchasing, project management, credit, customer service, warehouse, delivery, assembly, testing, and technical support, net of existing ERP, EDI, e-commerce, and rules-based automation, would materially move the estimate.
a.rationale: Electrical distribution has a meaningful digital information layer around product catalogs, substitutions, bids, orders, procurement, and customer service, but technical judgment and physical fulfillment remain substantial. The provided compensation-to-receipts ratio is only 10.32%, limiting the aggregate benefit even where tasks are exposed.

d5.bridge: EIA's AEO2026 says national electricity demand rose 2.1% annually over the preceding five years and projects 0.9%-1.6% annual growth through 2050, with data centers the dominant long-run driver and installed generating capacity increasing 50%-90% by 2050 across cases. DOE announced a $1.9 billion grid-upgrade opportunity on top of up to $10.5 billion in prior five-year grid-resilience funding. Wesco reported 2025 electrical-segment growth from data-center projects and infrastructure but a utility sales decline from reduced public-power activity. I translate these adjacent end-market signals into a five-year constant-quality service-basket ratio: electrification, data centers, grid capacity, reshoring, automation, and replacement demand support growth, while construction cycles, project delays, rates, public-power volatility, deflation, and channel pressure create downside. End-market load and public funding are not direct forecasts of merchant-wholesale volume, and Wesco's portfolio is broader than 423610.
caveats: Electricity demand, generating capacity, and federal funding are upstream demand proxies rather than direct merchant-wholesale service volumes.
AEO cases are alternative futures, not a single prediction, and project spending can lag load growth.
Nominal sales can rise from copper, tariffs, or equipment inflation without an equivalent increase in constant-quality service quantity.
d5.change_evidence: A five-year 423610 forecast decomposed into real unit volumes and price by utility T&D, data centers, nonresidential construction, OEM and automation, industrial MRO, lighting, and wire and cable, linked to distributor channel share and project backlogs, would materially narrow the range.
d5.rationale: The medium-term demand backdrop is favorable but uneven. Power generation and grid investment require substantial electrical equipment, and data-center and industrial projects require distribution and control products; however, distributors remain exposed to construction cycles, project timing, inventory corrections, and price deflation.

e.bridge: Census defines a principal merchant-wholesale activity with a broad construction, industrial, lighting, wire, and power-equipment basket. Wesco's electrical segment serves external construction, industrial, and OEM customers with technical products, project execution, supply-chain optimization, and advisory services, and it characterizes the market as highly fragmented with thousands of small, regional, and locally owned competitors. I translate that operating model into relatively high eligible-firm prevalence, discounted for thin commodity-only resellers, project-dependent firms, captive branches, excessive concentration, owner-dependent sales books, and businesses without a durable service or availability advantage. Wesco's breadth and scale may overstate service intensity in the LMM population.
caveats: Public evidence describes the industry activity and one large distributor, not the distribution of LMM firm quality.
The provided firm count is margin-bridged and may include firms with temporary project profits or weak normalized EBITDA.
Supplier authorization and local competitive structure can make apparently similar distributors economically different.
e.change_evidence: A census of LMM 423610 firms showing repeat-customer revenue, supplier-line authorization, stock versus direct-ship mix, end-market and project concentration, customer and supplier concentration, management depth, branch operations, and normalized EBITDA would materially move eligibility.
e.rationale: Many independent electrical distributors have recurring external customers, supplier authorizations, local inventory, technical knowledge, and service routines that constitute transferable operating businesses. The code is nevertheless heterogeneous, and not every nominal merchant has the customer diversity, management depth, or economics required by the lens.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 423610 ($21,563,384,273) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 423610 ($222,793,400,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1137 (margins.json[42]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Retail (Distributors) EBITDA/Sales 11.37%); revenue/employee $1,030,935 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 423610); class <5: 3,644 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,679,761 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $190,989 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 1,294 firms, avg revenue/firm $5,216,343 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $593,098 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 943 firms, avg revenue/firm $11,192,170 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,272,550 -> IN $1-10M band; class 20-99: 894 firms, avg revenue/firm $28,287,129 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $3,216,247 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 277 firms, avg revenue/firm $131,206,065 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $14,918,130 -> out of $1-10M band; class 500+: 192 firms, avg revenue/firm $717,371,953 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $81,565,191 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 1,837

o.bridge: Census defines 423610 around physical electrical construction materials, wiring supplies, lighting, and power equipment. Wesco documents local inventories, millions of products, technical expertise, project deployment, material management, supply-chain programs, product adaptation, delivery, testing, and complex project requirements. I translate those product, logistics, and accountability functions into high continuing operator need while allowing direct manufacturer sales, customer self-service, e-commerce, standardized procurement, and third-party logistics to reduce some distributor involvement. A large multinational's service portfolio may overstate operator intensity at smaller commodity-oriented merchants.
caveats: Operator requirement is distinct from the level of end-market demand captured in d5.
Large OEMs, utilities, and contractors can buy directly or automate replenishment for standardized products.
Supplier authorization, local availability, emergency response, and technical liability make disintermediation product-specific.
o.change_evidence: Channel-share data separating manufacturer-direct, merchant-distributor, agent, marketplace, contractor-direct, and customer-owned inventory by product and customer size, plus evidence on autonomous ordering and fulfillment adoption, would materially move this share.
o.rationale: AI can improve search, quotation, communication, and planning, but it cannot by itself take title, hold local inventory, preserve authorized supplier relationships, verify specifications, package and deliver materials, manage working capital, or stand behind project execution. Most surviving demand therefore still requires an accountable operator or operating organization.

caveats: No public evidence directly measures pass-through of AI savings in 423610.
Retention differs between commodity wire and conduit, engineered equipment, MRO availability, and value-added project services.
Commodity, tariff, freight, rebate, and inventory effects can swamp workflow savings in reported margins.
q.change_evidence: Customer- and SKU-level gross-profit waterfalls before and after automation, including bid win rates, account repricing, supplier rebates, freight, returns, service cost, project overruns, and renewal timing, would materially move retention.
q.rationale: Workflow savings can remain in distributor gross profit between bid, project, catalog, or account-price resets, especially when they improve response time, fill rates, technical service, or error avoidance. Retention is constrained because customers explicitly compare breadth, availability, service, proximity, and price; wire and conduit are commodity-price based; supplier increases are often passed through; and bids, large projects, buying groups, and e-commerce make prices contestable. Some savings will be reinvested in service levels, digital tooling, cybersecurity, and technical oversight.

caveats: No controlled AI implementation study specific to 423610 was found.
Large-company digital transformation may not be affordable or portable to small independent firms.
Incorrect substitutions, quantities, voltage ratings, certifications, or delivery promises can create outsized liability and require human approval.
rho.change_evidence: Independent before-and-after studies at electrical distributors showing workflow adoption, human review, cycle times, error and return rates, gross-profit impact, avoided hires, and implementation cost across quote-to-cash and procure-to-pay would materially change implementation expectations.
rho.rationale: Within five years, distributors can embed AI in product discovery, cross-reference and substitution support, quotation drafting, customer email, document extraction, order exception triage, purchasing analysis, demand forecasting, collections prioritization, and management reporting. Wesco's ongoing digital platform investment and expectation that digital tools improve efficiency and sales effectiveness support feasibility, while fragmented product data, legacy systems, supplier restrictions, electrical-code and specification risk, cybersecurity, customer-specific pricing, and warehouse integration constrain implementation.

s5.bridge: Gallup finds 22% of US employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer within five years, while McKinsey documents an aging owner base and that closure dominates observed SMB exits. I center the estimate below stated intent because planned transfers often fail, yet electrical distribution has strategic and geographic consolidation logic, supplier lines, customer books, inventory, and branch infrastructure that can attract trade buyers. The broad succession evidence may overstate completed deals and does not reveal industry-specific sponsor or strategic demand.
caveats: Owner-intent and owner-age evidence is broad rather than 423610-specific.
Supplier consent and line-card continuity can determine whether a transaction preserves the business.
Branch, asset, tuck-in, and internal-family transfers may not all meet the qualifying control-transfer definition.
s5.change_evidence: Verified closed-control transactions for LMM 423610 distributors from PitchBook, Capital IQ, GF Data, Axial, electrical-industry brokers, and supplier or buyer announcements, matched to the active eligible-firm denominator and excluding branch or inventory-only purchases, would materially move this probability.
s5.rationale: A fragmented base of local and regional private competitors creates a plausible acquisition pool, and durable electrical demand can support buyer interest. Successful transfer still depends on supplier approvals, customer retention, inventory quality, working capital, owner dependence, and normalized earnings.

principal_driver: High-volume product, quote, order, procurement, and customer-service information flows are well suited to AI assistance, while electrification creates a supportive demand backdrop.
principal_weakness: Competitive pricing, commodity exposure, project cyclicality, and a modest labor share cap retained value despite meaningful workflow exposure.

run_meta.title: Electrical Apparatus and Equipment, Wiring Supplies, and Related Equipment Merchant Wholesalers

sources.supports: Defines the industry as merchant wholesale distribution of electrical construction materials, wiring supplies, light fixtures and bulbs, and electrical power equipment used for generation, transmission, distribution, or control.
sources.title: Census industry profile: NAICS 423610 Electrical Apparatus and Equipment, Wiring Supplies, and Related Equipment Merchant Wholesalers

sources.supports: Reports the broad occupation mix including sales 22.11%, office and administrative support 15.27%, management 9.96%, production 7.97%, and transportation and material moving 26.54%.
sources.title: May 2023 OEWS: Merchant Wholesalers, Durable Goods, selected groups including NAICS 4236

sources.supports: Finds AI use concentrated in digital tasks, physical-labor categories least represented, 36% of occupations using AI for at least a quarter of tasks in the sample, and more augmentation than automation in observed conversations.
sources.title: Anthropic Economic Index: Mapping AI usage across occupational tasks

sources.supports: Describes electrical equipment, wire, cable, controls, utility hardware, technical and supply-chain services; a fragmented market with thousands of private competitors; digital efficiency investment; commodity-price and project risks; customer buying criteria; and 2025 demand from data centers and infrastructure.
sources.title: WESCO International, Inc. 2025 Form 10-K

sources.supports: Reports electricity-demand growth of 2.1% annually over the preceding five years, projects 0.9%-1.6% annual growth through 2050 across cases with data centers as the dominant driver, and projects installed generating capacity rising 50%-90% by 2050.
sources.title: EIA releases the Annual Energy Outlook 2026

sources.supports: Fall-2024 survey reports 22% of employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer within five years and 17% of owners age 55 or older planned a sale or transfer.
sources.title: Gallup: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan

sources.supports: Estimates more than half of US small-business owners are over 55 and reports that closure dominates observed SMB exits, indicating execution risk beyond stated transfer plans.
sources.title: McKinsey: Navigating the great small business ownership transition

sources.supports: Announces a 2026 grid-upgrade funding opportunity aimed at rising electricity demand and capacity needs and states that it follows up to $10.5 billion of five-year grid resilience and innovation funding.
sources.title: Department of Energy announces $1.9 billion investment in critical grid infrastructure

title: Electrical Apparatus and Equipment, Wiring Supplies, and Related Equipment Merchant Wholesalers
